sentences.text: We were on the train going to Saratov.
And I woke up in the middle of the night, and I find in my jacket a lot of stuff, and I didn't know what it is.
But I-- well, I didn't like it.
So when the train started to go, I got off the train.
My-- my friend shouldn't be able to get off the train.
I got off the train, and I went in on the train station.
They had a different toilet than we have in the United States.
It's built like a outhouse.
And I took stuff, all that stuff that I had, and I threw it in the in the outhouse.
And I pushed it down with a piece of wood.
Because I was scared the Russians would catch me like that.
I could be killed.
I didn't-- I saw passports, different things.
I didn't-- I was shaking so much.
I didn't even want-- I know it was stuff stolen out from other peoples on the train while they were sleeping from the luggage or whatever.
But it-- it shook me up.
I didn't care even to look if it's their diamonds or gold, whatever.
I just was interested to get rid of it.
And as I got rid of it, finally the KGB got me in the [INAUDIBLE], what is my doing there?
And I said I missed the train.
They interrogated me quite a while, but finally I went back on the train, and I went further.
As I got into Saratov, I got off the train in Saratov.
And the Russians appointed me to go to work in a collective called Iminia Krupskaya.
Iminia Krupskaya was Lenin's wife's name.
And I was working there for a long time.
And after a certain time, they said if want to go somewhere else, I can go.
Because undirectly they put me there to work.
Yes.
No.
No,
I didn't feel anything in Russia while I was in Russia, you know, like anti-Semitism, I personally didn't experience.
I didn't give them a reason to-- to do, because I-- I always did my job, what I had to do and, you know, didn't try somebody else to do the work for me.
Oh, we went on the field.
We were cutting-- what do you call that?
With the-- cutting the-- the time to take off the wheat, the corn from the field, we were cutting it with a tool, not like here.
You know, it was everything done by hand.
And I-- and then I went on the train, and I went to [INAUDIBLE].
I know I'm going to Tashkent.
And I'm sitting on the train, and I see people taking out food and eating, and my-- my tongue is hanging out to have something to eat.
But I had to go down a couple times and plain ask people to get-- for some bread, you know.
OK, and I finally got to Tashkent, and I got off in Tashkent on the train.
And I-- I see a lot of the refugees, because the climate is like-- is warm.
It stay all the time 70, 80 degrees.
So mostly a lot of refugees lined up there because it was warm.
One day, a man come survey, dressed in military, and asked, can anybody know how to finish wood?
And if you remember, I was staying.
I was working for the man who make caskets, and I did [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means polishing, put finish on, lacquer, whatever.
And I remembered that.
And I-- he asked me, and I said, I can do it.
And he said-- he took me away, and he said, we've got 1,000 sewing machines, and we have to make the-- make a uniform for the military, but we have to finish the tops.
I was lucky I remembered what to use, you know, alcohol, [INAUDIBLE], cola, everything, I knew it.
And I told him what I got.
But was I so good to do the job?
I don't think so, but I said, good.
Meanwhile, he set me up in a place.
He gave me food and everything.
And I picked myself one day, and I went out to the train.
And I asked some of these people, does anybody know out to finish furniture?
I happen to find two specialists who that was their living.
When I called them with me, and I was that time the boss already, and I-- and they started to work, and they finished five tops.
And it-- and they went back, and then I meet the guy, and I show it to him, and he's almost kissing me, never saw such a beautiful work done.
And he thinks I did it.
OK, he never paid me so much per square yard, or square foot.
I don't remember.
But it was a lot of money.
And he gave me a place where to live with a Russian family.
And as I was getting the money, I get a-- the Russian woman, I gave all the time quite a little money.
And I also brought the materials.
I-- I worked out all the time with some materials.
Because there are a lot of materials making uniforms.
And I took it with me and, I gave it to the lady.
And after everything was finished and I got all the money, she tells me there was a couple-- how do you know, I asked her, that these are KGB?
She said, I could recognize them a mile away.
They were looking for me.
And she said when they're looking for you, you've got problems.
So I picked myself up, and I went away from there, and I went into another collective.
Why do you think they were looking for you?
To-- to arrest me, take the money away.
But they needed me to do work.
They got me.
See, you're not allowed to exploit people.
In Russia at that time, you're not allowed to be a boss to people.
Everything works for the government.
But when they need me the job, they didn't ask the questions.
They need me to finish the job.
But when they finished, they were going to take the money and put me in jail.
I mean, I assumed.
And 99% I was right.
It would have happened that way.
But that's the way they did.
I moved there.
When I moved from there, somehow I saw on a train-- on a-- on a train station, I saw one of my brothers, Moishe.
And-- no, first I went-- first I went another place, and they got me not as-- from the-- the stuff I did, but to send me to Siberia.
They took a lot of refugees.

They needed work to be done-- not imprisonment, just to do work, to cut down trees, to cut down trees, and they would take the wood to make ammunition, you know, coal, to-- to fire, to make ammunition.
So I was there.
In Siberia.
In Siberia, I was maybe-- I don't remember-- about six months?
That was 1942, yes.
In 1942, I see the people going out to the woods to work, 20 people, and 10, 12 are coming back.
Eight are dying.
The food was-- I-- I was young at that time, but the people a little older than me were dying.
There was hardly any food.
It was just a piece of bread with a soup.
I cannot describe-- bad.
So it was-- it was terrible.
And I said, I have to get out of there.
I have to get out.
So a woman was there, and her husband was-- was-- was in another camp.
Her husband died.
And his name was [?
Calme ?] [?
Schweitzer. ?] So I said to her, can you get me the document, and I'll try to run away?
So I took the document, and I-- there's a train [INAUDIBLE].
And I went in in the train, and I worked myself up from that little village to Novosibirsk.
In Novosibirsk, I see on the train station is a general with a long coat.
And his hand is hanging on a-- what do you would call it?
That he was hid something.
And I'm looking at him, and-- and he looks at me, but I'm afraid.
And I still went to ask him, if you need any help to take in the luggage when the train comes to put it in in the train.
As I told him that, he said to me, something is bothering you.
If you want to tell me what it is, I'll try to help you.
He happened to be Jewish.
And-- and I told him the story what happened.
And he said to me, stay right here.
The train come, and I took the luggage in.
And he was on the first bench and then was another one on the top.
He told me to get up on the top.
I went up in the top, and I laid down, and he took his coat.
Because the Russian coats went down almost to the floor, maybe two, three inches from the floor.
And he covered me with that coat.
As the train was going, the KGB came in and checked him for documents.
Because to run-- to get away from Siberia, you cannot run away.
You have troubles.
And I remember he's telling the-- the people there, because he was a big general, I am [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means I got hit with a sharp nail, and there's something mentally wrong with me, not to bother me, to leave me alone.
And they left me alone.
And as we were going further, we're already out of the danger zone.
Soon we went out a couple hundred kilometers, miles, it started to-- no controls, everything was normal.
But to get out of Siberia was tough.
And he start to ask me-- he lives not far from Alma-Ata-- if I want to come and stay with him, because he's injured, and he's not going to the military.
He's going to stay home [INAUDIBLE].
And I didn't want to do that.
So I got off on a train.
And on the train, I see a brother of mine.
And then, there's a lot that people from my town.
This is at the-- at the train station?
At the train station.
Now I am in a town called-- if I'm not mistaken, it was called Turkistan, a town called Turkistan.
And we go into the town, and they're putting [? cars ?] in a collective, the Russians.
Yeah.
I happen to see my brother.
You're going to be hear a story what happened with my brother then.
I happen to see my brother.
And we all went in in that collective to work in a farm there.
And we didn't have food enough.
Should-- you would have to make a movie out of this.
We were crying.
We were hugging.
And not only with him, with the other people, too.
It was an excitement that I cannot describe you, because you're all alone, and suddenly you have people from your town and your brother.
That's the time that-- the last time when I saw him is when he came to Brest-Litovsk, and from Brest-Litovsk he went to [INAUDIBLE].
That's the time I didn't see him anymore.
And that was in 1939.
No, no, nobody, with.
Nobody.
Moishe.
No, nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
Now, I have to talk fast, because I want to get into that, which is going to be very exciting.
And we went to that [INAUDIBLE], that collective, and we were working there.
But we-- we didn't have food, so at night when we got out, we took chickens.
We killed the chickens.
We cooked the chickens.
We ate the chickens.
We went away in the field with [INAUDIBLE] to put the feathers and the bones in the dirt.
About 8 o'clock in the morning, 7 o'clock in the morning, the police comes over, check, and it's missing, the chickens.
Where's the chickens?
They're looking around for signs, bones, anything.
They couldn't find nothing.
I remember one time we went out, and we opened a magazine.
You know what a magazine-- a-- a supply place.
And it was a hill, a cemetery.
And we took out two bags of sugar.
And we buried it in the cemetery with plastic bags that they wouldn't get spoiled.
And we figured when it cools off in a couple weeks later, we'll go get the sugar.
We went back.
Nobody could find where the place was we buried, and we never find the place.
Then we went out, and we got-- got a cow in the-- way out in the field.
And we killed a cow.
And some of the guys were butchers.
They took the skin, and they put the skin in the ground.
We took wood with us, and put a lot of salt in the meat.
And we covered it up in the field with a lot of salt.
So often we went, we took a piece of meat, we cooked in the middle of the night, and we ate.
I'm going from there.
Suddenly comes the military in taking me and my brother and some other people.
And I got picture of two people.
They took quite a lot of people.
And they took him to send him to Siberia to do the work.
That's the second time I'm in Siberia.
Now I'm in a place called Beryozovka.
One guy-- while I'm working there, one guy gets very sick.
And there's a doctor about five kilometers, six kilometers away.
And-- and I was almost the youngest guy there.
The [INAUDIBLE] said I should go get the doctor.
I go get the doctor.
The doctor was a young girl.
I get the doctor, and you don't mind me to go into that conversation, because because as the doctor came and he gets some medicine.
But she has the medicine [? on. ?] So somebody has to go with her back to get the medicine.
Her name was Wanda [?
Verslev-- ?] no, not [?
Verslevka-- ?] Brulevska, Wanta Brulevska was the girl's name.
Everybody in the town kept saying that I should go because I slept with her.
OK, I went back there for the medicine.
She gave me the medicine.
She went with me back.
I gave the medicine back to the-- to the person who was sick.
She asked me to go back with her to the village, and I say, I cannot go, because I am working in the government.
She said, don't worry.
My-- my uncle is the one who has all this stuff sent from the United States.
And he would send from Siberia gold to the United States.
United States would send products.
And her father was the head-- uncle was the head of it.
She kept writing me papers that I'm sick and I shouldn't work.
I should be with her.
Anyways, it got to a point I was very scared.
I was scared, and I didn't want to go there.
But my brother kept after me.
My brother said, if you're not gonna do what you have to do, we're gonna die here.
There's no food.
There's hardly very little food, and we're not gonna survive.
So I went along with it.

One day, they come in, and they're telling me with a horse and wagon, and they're taking me-- me, my brother, and these two guys, which I got the picture, and a couple other guys, and they're taking us to that-- where she is.
You wouldn't believe it.
I get there.
There's a long table in a room with food, all kind of food, and I'm getting married without me even knowing that I am getting married.
And there's a-- there's a head of the-- of the town hall who's the one who gives you the-- the documents that I'm married.
I-- I eat.
I drink.
And I'm-- and I get so drunk that I get dizzy and they take me outside.
They put me on the grass outside.
People, you wouldn't believe it.
I must have been that time-- what?-- I was, oh, about 22 years old.
And one of our friend comes over-- no, OK, I'm going to eliminate, because she comes over, she's trying to kiss me while I'm laying there.
And-- and the marriage, that's it.
Then she wants to get me.
She talks to an officer.
I should stay there for certain days.
But the officer, everything went sour.
The officer said they're gonna arrest her because she's doing the wrong things.
So I went back to my village, but every two days--
Yeah, the marriage took place.
I'm married.
Listen to this.
I go back to my village.
And every two days, people coming over on a horse with a package.
We open up.
There's everything we wanted, sugar, salami, bread, butter, everything.
And we getting it.
And all the fellows think I am Christ, because I saved their life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But two weeks or maybe a month later, they took her away to [INAUDIBLE] because a train-- a plane, a train came with wounded soldiers, and she's a doctor and they needed her.
While she was gone, she has a friend who is very attractive.
And the friend made a party for me.
And the friend made a party for me, and I went there, and I had a lot of fun.
She went, the friend, to stay in a house, wrote me a letter, a love story letter.
Well, she didn't like it.
She ripped it up to pieces, and she put it in a-- in the stove, a wooden stove.
And she wrote me another letter.
But when this girl came back, and she opened the stove to make a fire, she sees the pieces of paper.
She takes the pieces of paper out, and puts it together and sees that was going on, hanky-panky.
Young I was and stupid I was.
When you're young, you're stupid most of the time.
Because your brains-- how do you say?
You-- your love controls you brains, not your brains controls your life.
And she got angry at me.
We were angry.
Meanwhile, Brulevska made a party, the other girl.
And I'm there, and I'm sitting.
And she runs over with a knife, and she wants to hurt me.
The girl who I married.
Yes.
I didn't consider my wife, but she was my wife.
To make the story short, my brother said to me, [?
Yankel, ?] Jack, it's gonna a miserable ending.
My advice would be to you to enlist immediately in the army.
At that time was all of us saying, anybody who comes from Poland and he wants to enlist in the Polish army, there's a woman general by the-- by the name called [?
Wanda ?] [?
Wasilewska. ?] And she's in Moscow, and she organizes army, a Polish army.
So that was the best thing for me.
I picked myself up, and I enlisted.
And I went to Novosibirsk, and from Novosibirsk, they send me to Moscow.
What did Wanta think of it?
Pardon me?
She didn't even know it what I'm doing.
I didn't discuss with her.
That was-- you know, it was a marriage, but it-- I don't know how to explain you.
I don't know.
It wasn't-- it wasn't comfortable, anyways.
And I went away to the military.
This was in 1943, yes-- 1943.
I'm in Moscow in the-- in the army, enlisted.
I-- you got to understand also it was not exactly a Polish army.
Because what it was, Stalin figured out if he takes-- they already chasing the Germans going back completely from Stalingrad, from almost near Moscow, Debin, everything.
The Germans are going back.
So what did he want?
He wanted the Polish people to start to work against the Germans.
It was-- I didn't have-- the only thing what I experienced very bad is when we got in Poland, a lot of Poles enlisted in the army.
And one day, I need a brush, and I go to the next truck.
In the compartment-- I was driving a GMC, a American truck that was sent to-- to Russia, Jeeps and GMCs.
And I see a letter.
And I see this letter stating, don't write, and don't let nobody know where you are and what you are.
I take this letter over to the Russian headquarters.
It turned out to be he was a guard in a concentration camp.
You follow me?
I think he changed his name or what and he went in and enlisted in the Polish army.
And that time, they took him away.
Because I knew right away that something's wrong.
Because when he got in in the army, he treated us like-- like he would be the head of the whole army, you know, very bad.
He tried to influence the army, all the [? volunteers. ?] And-- and that's the time [INAUDIBLE].
So that's-- then when I--
Yes.
Oh, that time I knew, because when we-- before we reached Berlin, I was in Lublin in the concentration camp.
I went in in the concentration camp.
I already knew what was going on in-- in-- we [? were in ?] a city--
Yeah, with-- with I was all the time with the army.
That was 1944.
Yes.
First of all, when I went in in the army, we started to move out from Moscow, yes?
We moved out from Moscow, and I was very lucky.
I got a very good job.
I got 20 trucks under me.
First I dove a general in a Jeep.
I was the chauffeur.
And I was driving-- and the reason I'm talking so fast, I'm letting out a lot of stuff.
We only got so much time.
So because what happened is I was a chauffeur for a general.
And the general liked me.
He wanted the camp to take out people to send to Stalingrad, where it was terrible there.
The general took me out.
And somehow I didn't go to Stalingrad, and I was staying in that battalion.
And then I got about 20 trucks.
What was my job?
My job was to move, transportation.
If the army kitchen is here, and the bakery, the kitchen, you know, so if the Germans were chased, we had to move another 20 miles.
So me and the trucks would do that.
We were told to deliver ammunition to the front line.
We would do almost-- all kind of work.
We would have to go to a train and take off 20-- 20 loads of wheat and bring it to-- to a mill where they make flours-- flour.
And we would do that.
So we always had something to do.
And I had to know this, [INAUDIBLE] told me all the time what I have to do.
Oh, sure.
OK, now I'm gonna go-- it's interesting enough, but I am rushing.
I'm driving once with three trucks.
And I want to smoke a cigarette.
But the wind is blowing, and I cannot light the cigarette.
So I stop in a site just to light the cigarette.
The other two trucks go in in the front of me.
And as they went in the front of me 1,000 feet, they were burning.
The Germans were on the top on the hill, and they were bombing the two trucks.
I turned around, and I went back.
That was my lifesaving.
OK, one time we went in with the trucks to pick up a kitchen, another group of military, and move it to another section.
I went in there and not knowing that the German army surrounded that place.
I don't know if you ever drove a car or a truck, and the more you want to run away, the-- the slower the truck goes because you're overflowing the gas in the-- in the truck.
So I back in to a-- a wooden fence to get out, and I went out.
Of the 18 trucks, about six of them survived.
Other [? one ?] was-- was cut off and-- and destroyed by the Germans.
There was a lot of coincidence like that.
So you had a lot of--
Where they bombed the bridges--
Very close.
One time, I am in a truck--
--with two soldiers, with two officers.
